coredata.dc:title: Non-enzymatic sensing of dopamine by localized surface plasmon resonance using carbon dots-functionalized gold nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Amiri, Mojtaba
Dadfarnia, Shayessteh
Haji Shabani, Ali Mohammad
Sadjadi, Sodeh
coredata.dc:description: A highly selective, sensitive, and rapid colorimetric sensor for the determination of dopamine (DA) was developed using the color change of S-doped carbon dots functionalized gold nanoparticles (S-CDs@Au NPs). The base of the method is the formation of a complex between the amine groups of dopamine with carboxylic groups of S-CDs@Au NPs followed by their aggregation with Fe3+ ions which acts as the linkers causing a red shift from 520 to 670 nm in the localized surface plasmon peak of S-CDs@Au NPs. The ratio of absorbance intensity at 670 nm to 520 nm was monitored as the analytical signal for determination of dopamine. The parameters affecting the analytical signal including reaction time, solution pH, the concentration of Au NPs and concentration of Fe3+ were optimized. At optimized conditions, the calibration curve was linear in the concentration range of 0.81–16.80 μM of dopamine. The detection and quantification limits were 0.23 μM and 0.77 μM, respectively. The intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviation (RSDs) at 5.0 μM of DA were 3.9% and 5.6%, respectively (n = 5). The applicability of the method for determination of DA in dopamine ampoule, urine and serum human samples was investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Colorimetric chemical sensor
Dopamine
Localized surface
Plasmon
Resonance
Gold nanoparticles

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of concentration of Au NPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the aggregation of S-CDs functionalized Au NPs by DA in the presence of Fe3+ ion.

TEM and DLS of Au NPs (I, III) and aggregated DA-S-CDs@Au NPs (II, IV); UV–vis absorption spectra of Au NPs before (Va) and after (Vb) addition of DA; FT-IR spectrum of S-CDs (VI); UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of CDs in aqueous solution (VII).
Absorption spectra of Au NPs modified with various CDs in the presence of DA and Fe3+.
Effect of pH on the aggregation of Au NPs. Conditions: amount of Au NPs 1 mL, [Fe3+] = 2.0 mg L−1, [DA] = 5.0 μM.
Effect of Au NPs concentration on the surface plasmon band intensity; Conditions: [Fe3+] = 2.0 mg L−1, pH = 4.0, [DA] = 5.0 μM.
Surface plasmon spectrum of bare Au NPs (A), Au NPs in the presence of CDs (B), Au NPs in the presence of CDs and DA (C), and Au NPs in the presence CDs, DA, and Fe3+ (D).

Surface plasmon spectrum of S-CDs@Au NPs in the presence of Fe3+ and different concentration (μM) of DA (A) and the calibration curve of DA under optimum conditions (B). Conditions: amount of Au NPs 1 mL, [Fe3+] = 2.0 mg L−1, [DA] = 5.0 μM.
Tolerance limits of interferences species in the determination dopamine (5 μM) at the optimum conditions.

row.entry: Mole ratio of interfering species to analyte
Aspartic acid, Tyrosine, Glycine, Oxalate, Lysine, Folic acid, Urea, K+, Na+, NH4 +, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination of dopamine in human urine and serum.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The results are mean of three measurements ± standard deviation; ND = Not detected.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the developed method with other colorimetric sensors for determination of dopamine.

row.entry: DA induces aggregation of the AHMT-Au NPs through hydrogen bonding interactions
The conformational change of aptamer facilitates salt-induced Au NP aggregation
Colorimetric sensing of DA based on the aggregation of Au NPs induced by copper ions
DA induces aggregation of the Au NPs based on core etching mechanism
Colorimetric sensing of DA based on the aggregation of S-CDs functionalized Au NPs by DA in the presence of Fe3+ ion

head.ce:title: Non-enzymatic sensing of dopamine by localized surface plasmon resonance using carbon dots-functionalized gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Yazd University, Safaieh, 89195-741, Yazd, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Material and Nuclear Fuel Cycle School, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Material and Nuclear Fuel Cycle School
Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly selective, sensitive, and rapid colorimetric sensor for the determination of dopamine (DA) was developed using the color change of S-doped carbon dots functionalized gold nanoparticles (S-CDs@Au NPs). The base of the method is the formation of a complex between the amine groups of dopamine with carboxylic groups of S-CDs@Au NPs followed by their aggregation with Fe3+ ions which acts as the linkers causing a red shift from 520 to 670 nm in the localized surface plasmon peak of S-CDs@Au NPs. The ratio of absorbance intensity at 670 nm to 520 nm was monitored as the analytical signal for determination of dopamine. The parameters affecting the analytical signal including reaction time, solution pH, the concentration of Au NPs and concentration of Fe3+ were optimized. At optimized conditions, the calibration curve was linear in the concentration range of 0.81–16.80 μM of dopamine. The detection and quantification limits were 0.23 μM and 0.77 μM, respectively. The intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviation (RSDs) at 5.0 μM of DA were 3.9% and 5.6%, respectively (n = 5). The applicability of the method for determination of DA in dopamine ampoule, urine and serum human samples was investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is the most important catecholamine neurotransmitter distributed in the brain tissues and body fluids of mammals. It has an important role in the function of several biological systems including the cardiovascular, hormonal and renal especially the central nervous system. A deficient level of dopamine in the brain leads to involuntary muscle activity in Parkinson’s disease whereas its excessive amount leads to Huntington’s disease [1]. Considering the critical function of dopamine in biological systems, many efforts have been made for detection of this biogenic amine [2,3]. Different methods have been used for determination of DA in biological fluids and pharmaceutical samples using electrochemical [4], UV–vis spectrophotometry [5], high performance liquid chromatography [6], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC/MS) [7], fluorometry [8] and capillary zone electrophoresis [9]. The electrochemical methods have the limitation of severe interference by the abundant electroactive materials such as ascorbic acid and uric acid [2]. Very low detection limits are achievable by some spectroscopic methods such as LC/MC, but they often require sample pretreatment, lengthy analysis time, and expensive equipment. Thus, it is still urgent to develop, a fast, low cost, facile, sensitive and selective method for the detection of DA in biologic samples. A great number of sensing platforms, including electrochemical [10,11], fluorescent [12] and colorimetric sensors [13–15] have been successfully developed for the determination of dopamine. Most of the reported dopamine colorimetric sensors are based on the use of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) [13–18]. The extensive use of Au NPs in biological science is due to their non-cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, unique physicochemical characteristics, good stability, tunable localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) and a high surface to volume ratio containing different functional groups suitable for interaction with biosystems. The color of the dispersed Au NPs depends on the size and the distance between them. Thus, the color of the well-dispersed Au NPs is red while those of completely aggregated Au NPs are blue. In recent years, several colorimetric sensors have been reported for quantification of dopamine based on Au NPs. Liu and co-workers [13] reported dithiobis(succinimidylpropionate) (DSP)-modified Au NPs as probes and ferric ions as cross-linkers for colorimetric visualization of dopamine. Feng and co-workers [14] used 4-amino-3-hydrazino-5-mercapto-1,2,4-triazol (AHMT) functionalized Au NPs as a probe for determination of dopamine in which DA acts as s molecular bridge causing the aggregation of the AHMT-Au and changing the color from red to blue which was used as the analytical signal. Zheng and co-workers [15] developed a colorimetric biosensor for detection of dopamine using dopamine-binding DNA aptamer (DBA) as recognition element and unmodified Au NPs as probes. Wen and co-workers [16] used β-cyclodextrin modified Au NPs (β-CD-Au NPs) as optical probes for colorimetric determination of DA based on controlled destabilization of β-CD-Au NPs and growth of Au assembling upon addition of analyte. Liu and co-workers proposed a colorimetric sensor [17] for measurement of dopamine based on the formation of Au core-Ag shell nanorods through reduction of Ag+ to Ag on the surface of gold nanorods by dopamine molecules. Thus, the surface modification of Au NPs has been improved the selectivity and sensitivity of determination of DA [13–16,18].

Furthermore, carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs) have revolutionized the sensing assays of biological compounds. CDs are a new class of nanoprobes with the particle size of less than 10 nm, widely applied in designing biosensors because of their unique properties such as water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, chemical stability, ease of bioconjugation, excellent optical properties, and simple synthesis. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no report on their use for modification of Au NPs.

In this work, the surface of Au NPs has been modified by S-CDs and used as a probe for colorimetric determination of DA. The method is based on the interaction of carboxylic groups on the surface of modified Au NPs with the amino groups of DA. The DA-S-CDs@Au NPs was aggregated in the presence of Fe3+ (as a linker) and a decrease in absorbance at the wavelength of 520 nm and appearance of a band at 670 nm was observed. The absorbance ratio at 670 to 520 nm was directly proportional to DA concentration in the aqueous solution which was used as the analytical signal. The affecting factors in the aggregation of Au NPs were optimized using the univariable method. The selectivity of the method was investigated through the analysis of the synthetic samples. The applicability of the developed method for the quantification of dopamine in real samples was also evaluated.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4.4H2O, 99.99%), phenylamine-4-sulfonic acid (C20H5S2N3), chitosan flakes of crab shells (practical grade >85% deacetylated; Brookfield viscosity >200,000 cps), NaCl, KCl, Na2CO3, CaCl2, ZnCl2, NH4Cl, and MgCl2 were provided from Sigma-Aldrich (Louis, MO, USA). Dopamine, uric acid, threonine, urea, glucose, folic acid, oxalate, histidine, tyrosine, glycine, tryptophan, aspartic acid, and lysine were supplied from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Deionized water was obtained from a Mill-Q water purification system (Bedford, MA). All the experiments were carried out at 25 ± 2 °C.

A Biowave II diode array spectrophotometer (WPA, Cambridge, UK) accompanied with 1.0 cm glass cuvettes was used for the absorbance measurement. The pH of the solutions was measured with a Metrohm pH meter (model 780, Switzerland). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was carried out with a Zeiss EM10C transmission electron microscope (Oberkochen, Germany) operating at an accelerating voltage 100 kV. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK) was used to measure the particle size and its distribution at room temperature. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Brucker Vector 22 spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) using KBr disks.

The gold nanoparticles with an average diameter of 13.0 nm were synthesized by the Wei et al. reported method [19]. Chitosan, a derivative of the polysaccharide chitin widely distributed in nature with a large number of the free amino and hydroxyl groups in its chains was selected as the reducing and stabilizing agent [20]. Briefly, 4 mL of 1.0 mM HAuCl4 was added to 10 mL of 34.6 mg mL−1 chitosan solution in 1% acetic acid under stirring at room temperature. After 15 min, the mixture was placed into a water bath at 95 °C for 1 h. In this stage, the color of the solution was changed from yellow to wine red indicating the formation of Au NPs. The resulted solution was cooled to room temperature which had a maximum absorption at 520 nm. This solution was stored at 4 °C for future use. The resulted solution had a maximum absorption at 520 nm. The Au NPs solution has approximately a concentration of 9.0 nM based on Beer’s law and extinction coefficient (ε) of ˜ 2.7 × 108 mol−1 L cm−1 at 520 [21].

Phenylamine-4-sulfonic acid was used for synthesizing of S-CDs using the previously reported hydrothermal method [22]. Thus, 1 g of phenylamine-4-sulfonic acid dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 8 h. Then, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the resulting black solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min and the large particles were removed. The supernatant was filtered through 0.2 μm microporous membrane followed by dialyzing in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) for 6 days (deionized water was replaced every 12 h).

1 mL of the prepared Au NPs, 0.5 mL of 50.0 μM S-CD along with 3 mL of distilled deionized water was transferred to a 5 mL volumetric flask. The mixture was shaken for few minutes to immobilize the S-CD on the surface of Au NPs. The pH was then adjusted to ˜ 4.0, and 10.0 μL of 1000 mg L−1 Fe3+ and 5.0 μL of DA solution was added to the flask and the volume was adjusted by distilled deionized water. In this step, the color of the solution was changed from wine red to bluish purple as detected by naked eyes. After 3 min, its absorbance spectrum was recorded and the intensity ratio of absorbance at 670 nm to 520 nm was used for the quantification of DA.

Pharmaceutical dopamine ampoule: The vial of dopamine ampoules (200 mg/5 mL) was prepared from Caspian Tamin Pharmaceutical Co. (Rasht-Iran). An exact volume of it was properly diluted with deionized water to get into the linear range of the designed method and was treated by the given procedure (Section 2.5).

Serum sample: A human serum sample was provided from a healthy volunteer who was not taken any medicine in the last three months. Firstly, 2 mL of human serum was spiked with different concentrations of dopamine followed by addition of 2 mL of acetonitrile. The mixture was vortexed for 1 min and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the serum protein residues [23]. The supernatant was carefully taken and treated according to the given procedure (section 2.5).

Urine sample: A human urine sample was obtained from a healthy volunteer who was not taken any DA in the past three months. The urine sample was subjected to a 100-fold dilution without any pretreatment [24]. Then, the diluted urine sample was spiked with different amount of DA and was used for analysis according to the given procedure of DA determination.

The prepared S-CDs contained abundant thiol functional groups interact with Au NPs through soft-soft acid-base interaction resulting in its surface modification. In the preliminary study, it was observed that addition of DA molecules followed by Fe3+ causes aggregation of DA-S-CDs@Au NPs resulting in a decrease in the LSPR band of modified Au NPs around 520 nm and the appearance of a new band at 670 nm. This observation is due to the assembly of DA molecules on the surface of S-CDs@Au NPs through the bonding between its primary amine with the carboxylic group of CDs and aggregation of DA-S-CDs@Au NPs by coordination of Fe3+ with DA molecules as demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Furthermore, it was found that the absorption ratio at 670 nm to 520 nm was directly proportional to the concentration of DA. So, it used as the analytical signal for the determination of DA.

The typical TEM, DLS images and the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra of Au NPs and aggregated DA-S-CDs@Au NPs are presented in Fig. 2 (IV), respectively. According to the TEM image and DLS analysis, the average diameter of Au NPs is about 13.0 nm (Fig. 2I, and III) while the aggregated DA-S-CDs@Au NPs have an average diameter of approximately 90.0 nm (Fig. 2II and IV). The aggregation of surface modified Au NPs in the presence of DA and Fe3+ species is further verified by the shift in the LSPR absorbance of Au NPs from 520 to 670 nm in (Fig. 2V). Furthermore, the synthesized S-CDs were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2VI). Accordingly, the peaks at around 1610, 1150, 780 and 790 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, CS, and aromatic C–H bonds, respectively. The broad and intense peak located at 3000-3500 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching mode of the OH group [25]. The absorption and emission spectra of the prepared S-CDs were also recorded (Fig. 2VII). The absorption spectrum of S-CDs aqueous solution contains three characteristic peaks at 335, 355 and 385 nm tailing into the visible range attributing to the π–π* transition of the CC bond, n– σ* transition of carboxyl or C–S bond and n– π* transition of the CO bond, respectively [25]. Furthermore, the S-CDs showed strong emission band centered around 465 nm (Fig. 2VII).

Unmodified Au NPs have been applied as a simple, fast, and label-free colorimetric detection of DNA, metal ions and some small molecules [26]. However, surface modified Au NPs had improved the sensitivity and selectivity of detection through specific interactions between receptors and analytes [18]. So, in order to investigate the effect of surface modification of Au NPs in the design of a sensor for determination of DA, in the preliminary studies, different carbon dots including N-CDs, P-CDs, S-CDs, and undoped CDs were synthesized using quinine, alendronate, phenylamine-4-sulfonic acid, and anthraquinone respectively. Then, their effect on the modification and aggregation of Au NPs in the designed system were considered. As it is demonstrated (Fig. 3 ), in the presence of DA and Fe3+, among different examined carbon dots modified Au NPs, only S-CDs modified Au NPs showed a new peak at 670 nm. This phenomenon can be related to the soft acid-base interactions of Au NPs with sulfur of CDs. Thus, S-CDs are immobilized on the surface of Au NPs and consequently their interaction with DA and Fe3+ resulted in the red shift in the color of solution and appearance of a peak at 670 nm due to the aggregation of Au NPs, while with other CDs the aggregation of Au NPs does not occur because of their weak interaction with Au NPs. Therefore, S-CDs modified Au NPs was selected for further studies.

Ionic strength may play an important role on the LSPR peak of NPs through its ability in constriction of the volume of electrical double-layer aroused from the capping agent [24]. The stability of the nanoparticles is usually evaluated by measuring their zeta potential [27]. The particles do not aggregate with zeta potential more positive than + 30 mV or more negative than -30 mV. Thus, NPs with ξ > +30 mV has strong repulsion force and are stable in solution. The zeta potential of the synthesized Au NPs stabilized with chitosan was found to be +46.5 ± 4.5 mV, reflecting a great electrostatic repulsion between them and consequently good dispersion in aqueous solution. This is in agreement with the previous study [27]. The stability of synthesized Au NPs was further confirmed by performing several experiments with varying concentration of NaCl (0.0–1.0) at constant conditions of other experimental factors except with no addition of Fe3+. It was found that chitosan stabilized Au NPs did not aggregate even at a concentration of 1.0 mol L−1 of NaCl. Thus, the developed probe is suitable for the determination of DA in media with high ionic strength.

The aggregation time is an essential factor affecting the speed of analysis. Thus, the absorbance spectrum of S-CDs@Au NPs was recorded after addition of Fe3+ and DA at a different time interval of 0–4 minutes. The signal reaches its maximum after 3 min indicating the complete aggregation of NPs. So, a time of 3 min was selected for aggregation of DA-S-CDs@Au NPs.

The pH of the solution can influence the form of the analyte in the solution, its interaction with NPs and linker ions, the surface charge of NPs as well as their aggregation. The most probable interaction between the S-CDs@Au NPs and DA occurs via the reaction of surface carboxylate groups and the amine of DA, while the interaction of DA molecule with the linker occurs through the coordination of its two vicinal deprotonated hydroxyl groups with Fe3+. Furthermore, the stoichiometry of the coordination of DA to Fe3+ is strongly affected by the pH of the solution [13]. Thus, the effect of pH on the analytical signal in the range of 2.0–10.0 was investigated. The result (Fig. 4 ) indicated that the analytical signal reaches its maximum in the pH range of 3.5−4.5. This can be related to the protonation of DA at the amine group facilitating its binding to carboxylate groups of the CDs surface and formation of bis complexes with the Fe3+ linker ions resulting in proper aggregation of DA-S-CDs@Au NPs. The lower response in pH < 3.5 can be attributed to the protonation of thiol groups of S-CDs declining its interaction with NPs resulting in a decrease in Au NPs aggregation and consequently the analytical signal. The decrease in the analytical signal at pH > 4.5 is due to the precipitation of the linker ion of Fe3+ as hydroxide species. Thus, subsequent work was performed at pH of ˜ 4.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of concentration of Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Au NPs concentration within the range of 0–10 nM on the analytical signal of the designed sensor was studied. It was found that (Fig. 5 ) the analytical response increases with an increase in Au NPs concentration reaching its maximum at 1.8 nM and leveled off at higher amounts. Thus, 1.8 nM of Au NPs was accepted as its optimum concentration.

The presence of Fe3+ ions as the linkers induces the aggregation and color change of S-CD@Au NPs through binding with the catechol group of DA. This interaction displays an intense absorption band in the visible region with a maximum at 670 nm which is detectable with the naked eyes. In the absence of Fe3+ ions, even at high concentration of DA, the aggregation of Au NPs was not observed (Fig. 6 A). Furthermore, the addition of S-CDs alone or along with DA did not result in the aggregation of NPs in the absence of Fe3 + (Fig. 6B and C). However, the addition of Fe3+ to the system induced aggregation of NPs resulting in the arose of a peak at 670 nm (Fig. 6D) which reaches its maximum at 2.0 mg L−1 of Fe3+. Thus, the concentration of Fe3+ must be at least 2.0 mg L−1 for obtaining high sensitivity.

The selectivity of the designed colorimetric sensor was evaluated by determining 5.0 μM of DA in the presence of various biomolecules and ions which were reported to interfere in DA determination. The results (Table 1 ) indicated that at the given mole ratio, the determination of the analyte is accurate and free from interferences.

The figures of merit of the method for the determination of DA were evaluated. A linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.81–16.80 μM of DA was obtained. The equation of calibration curve and determination coefficient were Y = 0.0718X + 0.2366 and 0.9983, respectively. In this equation, Y and X refer to the ratio of A670/A520 and concentration of DA (μM), respectively. The detection and quantification limits (defined as 3Sb/m and 10Sb/m) were found to be 0.23 μM and 0.77 μM, respectively. The intra- and inter-day relative standard deviations were determined through repetitive independent determination of 5.0 μM of DA (n = 5), and were found to be 3.9% and 5.6%, respectively Fig. 7 .

The capability of the developed sensor for the determination of DA in human urine and serum samples were examined. The accuracy of the method was evaluated through the recovery experiments of the spiked samples with different amount of DA. The results of this study (Table 2 ) revealed high recoveries of 98.0–102.0% illustrating good accuracy of the developed method. The accuracy of the method was further verified by analyzing the dopamine ampoule. The amount of DA in the ampoule was found to be 258 ± 7 mM which at 95% confidence level is in agreement with the claimed value of 261 mM. Thus, the method is suitable for the determination of DA in the biological and clinical samples.

In the present study, an effective sensor was developed for the colorimetric determination of DA. The bases of the sensor are the modification of Au NPs by S-CDs followed by interaction of carboxylic groups on the surface of modified Au NPs with the amino groups of DA and aggregation of DA-S-CDs@Au NPs in the presence of Fe3+ as the linker causing a color change from wine red to bluish purple. The ratio of the absorbance at 670–520 nm which was used as the analytical signal was proportional to DA concentration in the aqueous solution. The designed sensor is an effective candidate for the determination of DA due to its fascinating features such as biocompatibility, excellent stability, and simplicity in comparison to the existing methods. The other advantages of the method are the wide linear range, low detection limit, high selectivity, acceptable accuracy, and good reproducibility, without the requirement of expensive and complicated instruments. Furthermore, comparison of the figures of merit of the developed method with the previous methods for the quantification of DA (Table 3 ) revealed that besides simplicity, it has a wider linear range and a detection limit lower or comparable with others.
